docs/getting-started/planning.md:27:The general rule when determining what data to put into NetBox is this: If there's a model for it, it belongs in NetBox. For instance, NetBox has purpose-built models for racks, devices, cables, IP prefixes, VLANs, and so on. These are very straightforward to use. However, you'll inevitably reach the limits of NetBox's data model and question what additional data might make sense to record in NetBox. For example, you might wonder whether NetBox should function as the source of truth for infrastructure DNS records or DHCP scopes.

docs/integrations/rest-api.md:340:When retrieving devices and virtual machines via the REST API, each will include its rendered [configuration context data](../features/context-data.md) by default. Users with large amounts of context data will likely observe suboptimal performance when returning multiple objects, particularly with very high page sizes. docs/release-notes/version-3.3.md:309:Device type height can now be specified in 0.5U increments, allowing for the creation of devices consume partial rack units. Additionally, a device can be installed at the half-unit mark within a rack (e.g. U2.5). For example, two half-height devices positioned in sequence will consume a single rack unit; two consecutive 1.5U devices will consume 3U of space.

docs/release-notes/version-3.2.md:300:Several new models have been added to represent field-replaceable device modules, such as line cards installed within a chassis-based switch or router. Similar to devices, each module is instantiated from a user-defined module type, and can have components (interfaces, console ports, etc.) associated with it. These components become available to the parent device once the module has been installed within a module bay. This provides a convenient mechanism to effect the addition and deletion of device components as modules are installed and removed.
docs/release-notes/version-3.2.md:364:A new service template model has been introduced to assist in standardizing the definition and association of applications with devices and virtual machines. As an alternative to manually defining a name, protocol, and port(s) each time a service is created, a user now has the option of selecting a pre-defined template from which these values will be populated.

docs/administration/permissions.md:23:In addition to these, permissions can also grant custom actions that may be required by a specific model or plugin. For example, the `sync` action for data sources allows a user to synchronize data from a remote source, and the `render_config` action for devices and virtual machines allows rendering configuration templates.
docs/administration/permissions.md:93:Object-based permissions work by filtering the database query generated by a user's request to restrict the set of objects returned. When a request is received, NetBox first determines whether the user is authenticated and has been granted permission to perform the requested action. For example, if the requested URL is `/dcim/devices/`, NetBox will check for the `dcim.view_device` permission. If the user has not been assigned this permission (either directly or via a group assignment), NetBox will return a 403 (forbidden) HTTP response.

docs/release-notes/version-4.5.md:357:The previous many-to-one mapping of front to rear ports has been expanded to support bidirectional mappings. The `rear_port` and `rear_port_position` fields on the FrontPort model have been replaced with an intermediary PortMapping model, which supports any number of assignments between front port/position pair and a rear port/position pair. This change unlocks the ability to model complex inline devices that swap individual fiber pairs between cables.

docs/release-notes/version-3.5.md:286:This release introduces the ability to render device configurations from Jinja2 templates natively within NetBox, via both the UI and REST API. The new [ConfigTemplate](../models/extras/configtemplate.md) model stores template code (which may be defined locally or sourced from remote data files). The rendering engine passes data gleaned from both config contexts and request parameters to generate complete configurations suitable for direct application to network devices.

docs/models/extras/configtemplate.md:3:Configuration templates can be used to render [device](../dcim/device.md) configurations from [context data](../../features/context-data.md). Templates are written in the [Jinja2 language](https://jinja.palletsprojects.com/) and can be associated with devices roles, platforms, and/or individual devices.
docs/models/extras/configtemplate.md:5:Context data is made available to [devices](../dcim/device.md) and/or [virtual machines](../virtualization/virtualmachine.md) based on their relationships to other objects in NetBox. For example, context data can be associated only with devices assigned to a particular site, or only to virtual machines in a certain cluster.

docs/models/extras/tag.md:25:The assignment of a tag may be limited to a prescribed set of objects. For example, it may be desirable to limit the application of a specific tag to only devices and virtual machines.
docs/models/extras/savedfilter.md:3:When filtering lists of objects in NetBox, users can save applied filters for future use. This is handy for complex filter strategies involving multiple discrete filters. For example, you might want to find all planned devices within a region that have a specific platform. Once you've applied the desired filters to the object list, simply create a saved filter with name and optional description. This filter can then be applied directly for future queries via both the UI and REST API.

docs/models/dcim/interface.md:3:Interfaces in NetBox represent network interfaces used to exchange data with connected devices. On modern networks, these are most commonly Ethernet, but other types are supported as well. IP addresses and VLANs can be assigned to interfaces.
docs/models/dcim/interface.md:9:    Although both devices and virtual machines both can have interfaces assigned, a separate model is used for each. Thus, device interfaces have some properties that are not present on virtual machine interfaces and vice versa.
docs/models/dcim/device.md:3:Every piece of hardware which is installed within a site or rack exists in NetBox as a device. Devices are measured in rack units (U) and can be half depth or full depth. A device may have a height of 0U: These devices do not consume vertical rack space and cannot be assigned to a particular rack unit. A common example of a 0U device is a vertically-mounted PDU.
docs/models/dcim/device.md:19:The device's configured name. This field is optional; devices can be unnamed. However, if set, the name must be unique to the assigned site and tenant.
docs/models/dcim/device.md:96:If this device will serve as a host for a virtualization [cluster](../virtualization/cluster.md), it can be assigned here. (Host devices can also be assigned by editing the cluster.)
netbox/dcim/object_actions.py:13:    Add components to the selected devices.
docs/models/dcim/poweroutlet.md:3:Power outlets represent the outlets on a power distribution unit (PDU) or other device that supplies power to dependent devices. Each power port may be assigned a physical type, and may be associated with a specific feed leg (where three-phase power is used) and/or a specific upstream power port. This association can be used to model the distribution of power within a device.
netbox/templates/extras/panels/configcontext_assignment.html:6:    {% for title, objects in assigned_objects %}
docs/models/dcim/platform.md:3:A platform defines the type of software running on a [device](./device.md) or [virtual machine](../virtualization/virtualmachine.md). This can be helpful to model when it is necessary to distinguish between different versions or feature sets. Note that two devices of the same type may be assigned different platforms: For example, one Juniper MX240 might run Junos 14 while another runs Junos 15.
docs/models/dcim/platform.md:7:Platforms may optionally be limited by [manufacturer](./manufacturer.md): If a platform is assigned to a particular manufacturer, it can only be assigned to devices with a type belonging to that manufacturer.
docs/models/dcim/platform.md:9:The assignment of platforms to devices and virtual machines is optional.
docs/models/dcim/platform.md:29:If designated, this platform will be available for use only to devices assigned to this [manufacturer](./manufacturer.md). This can be handy e.g. for limiting network operating systems to use on hardware produced by the relevant vendor. However, it should not be used when defining general-purpose software platforms.
docs/models/dcim/platform.md:33:The default [configuration template](../extras/configtemplate.md) for devices assigned to this platform.
docs/models/dcim/virtualchassis.md:3:A virtual chassis represents a set of devices which share a common control plane. A common example of this is a stack of switches which are connected and configured to operate as a single managed device. Each device in the virtual chassis is referred to as a VC member, and assigned a position and (optionally) a priority. VC member devices commonly reside within the same rack, though this is not a requirement.
docs/models/dcim/virtualchassis.md:5:One of the member devices may be designated as the VC master: This device will typically be assigned a name, services, virtual interfaces, and other attributes related to managing the VC.  If a VC master is defined, interfaces from all VC members are displayed when navigating to its device interfaces view. This does not include management-only interfaces belonging to other members.
docs/models/dcim/virtualchassis.md:8:    It's important to recognize the distinction between a virtual chassis and a chassis-based device. A virtual chassis is **not** suitable for modeling a chassis-based switch with removable line cards (such as the Juniper EX9208), as its line cards are _not_ physically autonomous devices. Instead, use [modules](./module.md) for these.
docs/models/dcim/virtualchassis.md:18:The domain assigned for VC member devices.
docs/models/dcim/devicerole.md:29:The default [configuration template](../extras/configtemplate.md) for devices assigned to this role.

docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:5:Device types are instantiated as devices installed within sites and/or equipment racks. For example, you might define a device type to represent a Juniper EX4300-48T network switch with 48 Ethernet interfaces. You can then create multiple _instances_ of this type named "switch1," "switch2," and so on. Each device will automatically inherit the components (such as interfaces) of its device type at the time of creation. However, changes made to a device type will **not** apply to instances of that device type retroactively.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:8:    This parent/child relationship is **not** suitable for modeling chassis-based devices, wherein child members share a common control plane. Instead, line cards and similarly non-autonomous hardware should be modeled as modules or inventory items within a device.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:12:When adding component templates to a device type, the string `{vc_position}` can be used in component template names to reference the
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:13:`vc_position` field of the device being provisioned, when that device is a member of a Virtual Chassis.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:15:For example, an interface template named `Gi{vc_position}/0/0` installed on a Virtual Chassis
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:18:If the device is not a member of a Virtual Chassis, `{vc_position}` defaults to `0`. A custom
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:19:fallback value can be specified using the syntax `{vc_position:X}`, where `X` is the desired default.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:20:For example, `{vc_position:1}` will render as `1` when no Virtual Chassis position is set.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:38:If defined, devices instantiated from this type will automatically inherit the selected platform. (This assignment can be changed after the device has been created.)
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:54:Indicates whether this is a parent type (capable of housing child devices), a child type (which must be installed within a device bay), or neither.
docs/models/dcim/devicetype.md:58:The default direction in which airflow circulates within the device chassis. This may be configured differently for instantiated devices (e.g. because of different fan modules).
docs/models/dcim/modulebay.md:6:    If you need to model child devices rather than modules, use a [device bay](./devicebay.md) instead.
docs/models/dcim/location.md:3:[Racks](./rack.md) and [devices](./device.md) can be grouped by location within a [site](./site.md). A location may represent a floor, room, cage, or similar organizational unit. Locations can be nested to form a hierarchy. For example, you may have floors within a site, and rooms within a floor.
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:24:Similarly, the string `{vc_position}` can be used in component template names to reference the
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:25:`vc_position` field of the device being provisioned, when that device is a member of a Virtual Chassis.
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:27:For example, an interface template named `Gi{vc_position}/{module}/0` installed on a Virtual Chassis
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:30:If the device is not a member of a Virtual Chassis, `{vc_position}` defaults to `0`. A custom
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:31:fallback value can be specified using the syntax `{vc_position:X}`, where `X` is the desired default.
docs/models/dcim/moduletype.md:32:For example, `{vc_position:1}` will render as `1` when no Virtual Chassis position is set.

docs/models/dcim/devicebay.md:3:Device bays represent a space or slot within a parent device in which a child device may be installed. For example, a 2U parent chassis might house four individual blade servers. The chassis would appear in the rack elevation as a 2U device with four device bays, and each server within it would be defined as a 0U device installed in one of the device bays. Child devices do not appear within rack elevations or count as consuming rack units.
docs/models/dcim/devicebay.md:5:Child devices are first-class Devices in their own right: That is, they are fully independent managed entities which don't share any control plane with the parent.  Just like normal devices, child devices have their own platform (OS), role, tags, and components.  LAG interfaces may not group interfaces belonging to different child devices.

docs/models/dcim/rack.md:3:The rack model represents a physical two- or four-post equipment rack in which [devices](./device.md) can be installed. Each rack must be assigned to a [site](./site.md), and may optionally be assigned to a [location](./location.md) within that site. Racks can also be organized by user-defined functional roles or by [rack groups](./rackgroup.md). The name and facility ID of each rack within a location must be unique.

docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:3:A virtual machine (VM) represents a virtual compute instance hosted within a cluster or directly on a device. Each VM must be assigned to at least one of: a [site](../dcim/site.md), a [cluster](./cluster.md), or a [device](../dcim/device.md).
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:13:- If assigned to a **cluster**: unique within the cluster and tenant.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:14:- If assigned to a **device** (no cluster): unique within the device and tenant.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:44:- **Site only**: The VM exists at a site but is not assigned to a specific cluster or device.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:45:- **Cluster only**: The VM belongs to a virtualization cluster. The site is automatically inferred from the cluster's scope.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:46:- **Device only**: The VM runs directly on a physical host device without a cluster (e.g. containers). The site is automatically inferred from the device's site.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:47:- **Cluster + Device**: The VM belongs to a cluster and is pinned to a specific host device within that cluster. The device must be a registered host of the assigned cluster.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:50:    Virtual machines can now be assigned directly to a device without requiring a cluster. This is particularly useful for modeling VMs running on standalone hosts outside of a cluster.
docs/models/virtualization/virtualmachine.md:83:    Unlike devices, uniqueness is not enforced for virtual machine serial numbers.
docs/models/dcim/rackrole.md:3:Each rack can optionally be assigned a user-defined functional role. For example, you might designate a rack for compute or storage resources, or to house colocated customer devices.
docs/models/dcim/powerport.md:35:    When creating a power port on a device which is mapped to outlets and supplies power to downstream devices, the maximum and allocated draw numbers should be left blank. Utilization will be calculated by taking the sum of all power ports of devices connected downstream.
docs/models/virtualization/clustergroup.md:3:Cluster groups may be created for the purpose of organizing [clusters](./cluster.md). The arrangement of clusters into groups is optional.
docs/models/dcim/consoleserverport.md:3:A console server is a device which provides remote access to the local consoles of connected devices. They are typically used to provide remote out-of-band access to network devices, and generally connect to [console ports](./consoleport.md).

docs/models/dcim/module.md:5:Similar to devices, modules are instantiated from [module types](./moduletype.md), and any components associated with the module type are automatically instantiated on the new model. Each module must be installed within a [module bay](./modulebay.md) on a [device](./device.md), and each module bay may have only one module installed in it.
docs/models/virtualization/cluster.md:3:A cluster is a logical grouping of physical resources within which [virtual machines](./virtualmachine.md) run. Physical [devices](../dcim/device.md) may be associated with clusters as hosts. This allows users to track on which host(s) a particular virtual machine may reside.
docs/models/virtualization/cluster.md:9:A human-friendly name for the cluster. Must be unique within the assigned group and site.
docs/models/virtualization/cluster.md:13:The [cluster type](./clustertype.md) assigned for this cluster.

docs/models/virtualization/cluster.md:21:The cluster's operational status.

docs/introduction.md:63:Careful consideration has been given to the data model to ensure that it can accurately reflect a real-world network. For instance, IP addresses are assigned not to devices, but to specific interfaces attached to a device, and an interface may have multiple IP addresses assigned to it.

docs/features/ipam.md:67:NetBox models network applications as discrete service objects associated with devices and/or virtual machines, and optionally with specific IP addresses attached to those parent objects. These can be used to catalog the applications running on your network for reference by other objects or integrated tools.

docs/features/context-data.md:3:Configuration context data (or "config contexts" for short) is a powerful feature that enables users to define arbitrary data that applies to device and virtual machines based on certain characteristics. For example, suppose you want to define syslog servers for devices assigned to sites within a particular region. In NetBox, you can create a config context instance containing this data and apply it to the desired region. All devices within this region will now include this data when fetched via an API.
docs/features/context-data.md:40:For example, suppose we want to specify a set of syslog and NTP servers for all devices within a region. We could create a config context instance with a weight of 1000 assigned to the region, with the following JSON data:
docs/features/context-data.md:86:    If you find that you're routinely defining local context data for many individual devices or virtual machines, [custom fields](./customization.md#custom-fields) may offer a more effective solution.
docs/features/event-rules.md:15:An event rule may include a set of conditional logic expressed in JSON used to control whether an event triggers for a specific object. For example, you may wish to trigger an event for devices only when the `status` field of an object is "active":
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:31:A manufacturer generally represents an organization which produces hardware devices. These can be defined by users, however they should represent an actual entity rather than some abstract idea.
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:35:A device type represents a unique combination of manufacturer and hardware model which maps to discrete make and model of device which exists in the real world. Each device type typically has a number of components created on it, representing network interfaces, device bays, and so on. New devices of this type can then be created in NetBox, and any associated components will be automatically replicated from the device type. This avoids needing to tediously recreate components for each device as it is added in NetBox.
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:49:* Device bays (which house child devices)
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:61:    The instantiation of components from a device type definition occurs only at the time of device creation. If you modify the components assigned to a device type, it will not affect devices which have already been created. This guards against any inadvertent changes to existing devices. However, you always have the option of adding, modifying, or deleting components on existing devices. (These changes can easily be applied to multiple devices at once using the bulk operations available in the UI.)
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:71:Sometimes it is necessary to model a set of physical devices as sharing a single management plane. Perhaps the most common example of such a scenario is stackable switches. These can be modeled as virtual chassis in NetBox, with one device acting as the chassis master and the rest as members. All components of member devices will appear on the master.
docs/features/devices-cabling.md:79:Much like device types and devices, module types can instantiate discrete modules, which are hardware components installed within devices. Modules often have their own child components, which become available to the parent device. For example, when modeling a chassis-based switch with multiple line cards in NetBox, the chassis would be created (from a device type) as a device, and each of its line cards would be instantiated from a module type as a module installed in one of the device's module bays.
docs/features/virtualization.md:3:Virtual machines, clusters, and standalone hypervisors can be modeled in NetBox alongside physical infrastructure. IP addresses and other resources are assigned to these objects just like physical objects, providing a seamless integration between physical and virtual networks.

docs/features/virtualization.md:26:A cluster is one or more physical host devices on which virtual machines can run.
docs/features/virtualization.md:28:Each cluster must have a type and operational status, and may be assigned to a group. (Both types and groups are user-defined.) Each cluster may designate one or more devices as hosts, however this is optional.
docs/features/virtualization.md:43:- Assigned to a cluster and optionally pinned to a specific host device within that cluster.
docs/features/virtualization.md:44:- Assigned directly to a standalone device that does not belong to any cluster.

docs/features/power-tracking.md:17:A device power port can be connected to a power feed via a cable. Only one port can be connected to a feed: Where multiple devices draw power from the same feed, a power distribution unit (PDU) must be modeled as an individual device mapping a power port to multiple power outlets to which the downstream devices can connect (as in the example above).
docs/features/customization.md:11:Objects can be filtered by the tags they have applied. For example, the following API request will retrieve all devices tagged as "monitored":
docs/features/customization.md:14:GET /api/dcim/devices/?tag=monitored
docs/features/customization.md:20:GET /api/dcim/devices/?tag=monitored&tag=deprecated
docs/features/tenancy.md:22:Most core objects within NetBox can be assigned to a particular tenant, so this model provides a very convenient way to correlate resource allocation across object types. For example, each of your customers might have its own racks, devices, IP addresses, circuits and so on: These can all be easily tracked via tenant assignment.
docs/features/facilities.md:71:Finally, NetBox models each equipment rack as a discrete object within a site and location. These are physical objects into which devices are installed. Each rack can be assigned an operational status, type, facility ID, and other attributes related to inventory tracking. Each rack also must define a height (in rack units) and width, and may optionally specify its physical dimensions.
docs/features/facilities.md:73:Each rack must be associated to a site, but the assignment to a location or rack group within that site is optional. Users can also create custom roles to which racks can be assigned. NetBox supports tracking rack space in half-unit increments, so it's possible to mount devices at e.g. position 2.5 within a rack.
docs/features/facilities.md:76:    You'll notice in the diagram above that a device can be installed within a site, location, or rack. This approach affords plenty of flexibility as not all sites need to define child locations, and not all devices reside in racks.

docs/features/authentication-permissions.md:12:The implementation of constrains is what enables NetBox administrators to assign per-object permissions: Users can be limited to viewing or interacting with arbitrary subsets of objects based on the objects' attributes. For example, you might restrict a particular user to viewing only those prefixes or IP addresses within a particular VRF. Or you might restrict a group to modifying devices within a particular region.

 /** @internal returns the passed element if vertically scrollable, else the closest parent that will, up to the entire document scrolling element */\n  static getScrollElement(el?: HTMLElement): `resizestop`: Called after user is done resizing the item, with updated DOM attributes\n   *\n   * @param name event name(s) to listen for (space separated for multiple)\n   * @param callback function to call when event occurs\n   * @returns the grid instance for chaining\n   *\n   * @example\n   * // Listen to multiple events at once\n   * grid.on('added removed change', (event, items) => {\n   *   items.forEach(item => console.log('Item changed:', item));\n   * });\n   *\n   * // Listen to individual events\n   * grid.on('added', (event, items) => {\n   *   items.forEach(item => console.log('Added item:', item));\n   * });\n   */\n  public on(name: 'dropped', callback: GridStackDroppedHandler): GridStack\n  public on(name: 'enable' | 'disable', callback: GridStackEventHandler): GridStack\n  public on(name: 'change' | 'added' | 'removed' | 'resizecontent', callback: GridStackNodesHandler): GridStack\n  public on(name: 'resizestart' | 'resize' | 'resizestop' | 'dragstart' | 'drag' | 'dragstop', callback: GridStackElementHandler): GridStack\n  public on(name: string, callback: GridStackEventHandlerCallback): GridStack\n  public on(name: GridStackEvent | string, callback: GridStackEventHandlerCallback): GridStack {\n    // check for array of names being passed instead\n    if (name.indexOf(' ') !== -1) {\n      const names = name.split(' ') as GridStackEvent[];\n      names.forEach(name => this.on(name, callback));\n      return this;\n    }\n\n    // native CustomEvent handlers -   1237	        # Clear vc_position and vc_priority on member devices BEFORE calling super().delete()

  1405	        if self._original_assigned_object_id and self._original_assigned_object_type_id:

  1407	            ct = ContentType.objects.get_for_id(self._original_assigned_object_type_id)
  1408	            original_assigned_object = ct.get_object_for_this_type(pk=self._original_assigned_object_id)

  1418	                if original_assigned_object != assigned_object:

   162	    """
   163	    An abstract model inherited by all models to which a Cable can terminate. Provides the `cable` and `cable_end`
   164	    fields for caching cable associations, as well as `mark_connected` to designate "fake" connections.
   165	    """

   185	            MaxValueValidator(CABLE_CONNECTOR_MAX)
   186	        ),
   187	    )
   188	    cable_positions = ArrayField(
   189	        base_field=models.PositiveSmallIntegerField(
   190	            validators=(
   191	                MinValueValidator(CABLE_POSITION_MIN),
   192	                MaxValueValidator(CABLE_POSITION_MAX)
   193	            )
   194	        ),
   195	        blank=True,

   197	    )

   204	    cable_terminations = GenericRelation(
   205	        to='dcim.CableTermination',

   207	        object_id_field='termination_id',
   208	        related_query_name='%(class)s',
   209	    )

   211	    class Meta:
   212	        abstract = True

   214	    def clean(self):
   215	        super().clean()
   216	
   217	        if self.cable:
   218	            if not self.cable_end:
   219	                raise ValidationError({
   220	                    "cable_end": _("Must specify cable end (A or B) when attaching a cable.")
   221	                })
   222	            if self.cable_connector and not self.cable_positions:
   223	                raise ValidationError({
   224	                    "cable_positions": _("Must specify position(s) when specifying a cable connector.")
   225	                })
   226	            if self.cable_positions and not self.cable_connector:
   227	                raise ValidationError({
   228	                    "cable_positions": _("Cable positions cannot be set without a cable connector.")
   229	                })
   230	            if self.mark_connected:
   231	                raise ValidationError({
   232	                    "mark_connected": _("Cannot mark as connected with a cable attached.")
   233	                })
   234	        else:
   235	            if self.cable_end:
   236	                raise ValidationError({
   237	                    "cable_end": _("Cable end must not be set without a cable.")
   238	                })
   239	            if self.cable_connector:
   240	                raise ValidationError({
   241	                    "cable_connector": _("Cable connector must not be set without a cable.")
   242	                })
   243	            if self.cable_positions:
   244	                raise ValidationError({
   245	                    "cable_positions": _("Cable termination positions must not be set without a cable.")
   246	                })

   248	    @property
   249	    def link(self):
   250	        """
   251	        Generic wrapper for a Cable, WirelessLink, or some other relation to a connected termination.
   252	        """
   253	        return self.cable
   254	
   255	    @cached_property
   256	    def link_peers(self):
   257	        if not self.cable:
   258	            return []
   259	
   260	        if self.cable.profile:
   261	            return self._get_profile_link_peers()
   262	
   263	        return [peer.termination for peer in self.cable.terminations.all() if peer.cable_end != self.cable_end]
   264	
   265	    def _get_profile_link_peers(self):
   266	        if self.cable_end is None or self.cable_connector is None or not self.cable_positions:
   267	            return []
   268	
   269	        profile = self.cable.profile_class()
   270	        peer_terminations = {
   271	            (peer.connector, position): peer.termination
   272	            for peer in self.cable.terminations.all()
   273	            if peer.cable_end == self.opposite_cable_end and peer.connector is not None
   274	            for position in peer.positions or []
   275	        }
   276	        link_peers = []
   277	
   278	        for position in self.cable_positions:
   279	            mapped_position = profile.get_mapped_position(self.cable_end, self.cable_connector, position)
   280	            if mapped_position is None:
   281	                continue
   282	
   283	            peer = peer_terminations.get(mapped_position)
   284	            if peer is not None and peer not in link_peers:
   285	                link_peers.append(peer)
   286	
   287	        return link_peers

   295	        raise NotImplementedError(
   296	            _("{class_name} models must declare a parent_object property").format(class_name=self.__class__.__name__)
   297	        )

   299	    @property
   300	    def opposite_cable_end(self):
   301	        if not self.cable_end:
   302	            return None
   303	        return CableEndChoices.SIDE_A if self.cable_end == CableEndChoices.SIDE_B else CableEndChoices.SIDE_B

   306	        """Save attributes from the given CableTermination on the terminating object."""

   308	        self.cable_end = termination.cable_end
   309	        self.cable_connector = termination.connector
   310	        self.cable_positions = termination.positions
   311	    set_cable_termination.alters_data = True

   313	    def clear_cable_termination(self, termination):
   314	        """Clear all cable termination attributes from the terminating object."""
   315	        self.cable = None
   316	        self.cable_end = None
   317	        self.cable_connector = None
   318	        self.cable_positions = None
   319	    clear_cable_termination.alters_data = True

   322	class PathEndpoint(models.Model):
   323	    """
   324	    An abstract model inherited by any CabledObjectModel subclass which represents the end of a CablePath; specifically,
   325	    these include ConsolePort, ConsoleServerPort, PowerPort, PowerOutlet, Interface, and PowerFeed.
   326	
   327	    `_path` references the CablePath originating from this instance, if any. It is set or cleared by the receivers in
   328	    dcim.signals in response to changes in the cable path, and complements the `origin` GenericForeignKey field on the
   329	    CablePath model. `_path` should not be accessed directly; rather, use the `path` property.
   330	
   331	    `connected_endpoints()` is a convenience method for returning the destination of the associated CablePath, if any.
   332	    """
   333	
   334	    _path = models.ForeignKey(
   335	        to='dcim.CablePath',
   336	        on_delete=models.SET_NULL,
   337	        null=True,
   338	        blank=True,
   339	    )
   340	
   341	    class Meta:
   342	        abstract = True
   343	
   344	    def trace(self):
   345	        origin = self
   346	        path = []
   347	
   348	        # Construct the complete path (including e.g. bridged interfaces)
   349	        while origin is not None:
   350	            # Go through the public accessor rather than dereferencing `_path`
   351	            # directly. During cable edits, CablePath rows can be deleted and
   352	            # recreated while this endpoint instance is still in memory.
   353	            cable_path = origin.path
   354	            if cable_path is None:
   355	                break
   356	
   357	            path.extend(cable_path.path_objects)
   358	
   359	            # If the path ends at a non-connected pass-through port, pad out the link and far-end terminations
   360	            if len(path) % 3 == 1:
   361	                path.extend(([], []))
   362	            # If the path ends at a site or provider network, inject a null "link" to render an attachment
   363	            elif len(path) % 3 == 2:
   364	                path.insert(-1, [])
   365	
   366	            # Check for a bridged relationship to continue the trace.
   367	            destinations = cable_path.destinations
   368	            if len(destinations) == 1:
   369	                origin = getattr(destinations[0], 'bridge', None)
   370	            else:
   371	                origin = None
   372	
   373	        # Return the path as a list of three-tuples (A termination(s), cable(s), B termination(s))
   374	        return list(zip(*[iter(path)] * 3))
   375	
   376	    @property
   377	    def path(self):
   378	        """
   379	        Return this endpoint's current CablePath, if any.
   380	
   381	        `_path` is a denormalized reference that is updated from CablePath
   382	        save/delete handlers, including queryset.update() calls on origin
   383	        endpoints. That means an already-instantiated endpoint can briefly hold
   384	        a stale in-memory `_path` relation while the database already points to
   385	        a different CablePath (or to no path at all).
   386	
   387	        Two stale cases are repaired by refreshing only the `_path` field
   388	        from the database:
   389	
   390	        1. The endpoint is linked (by cable or wireless link) but `_path` is
   391	           unset, because the instance was loaded before its path was traced
   392	           (e.g. while queued for event serialization during link creation).
   393	        2. The cached relation points to a CablePath row that has just been
   394	           deleted.
   395	
   396	        Repairing case 1 costs one query per access for a linked endpoint
   397	        whose path is genuinely absent in the database. That state is
   398	        transient outside of tracing failures, so no result caching is
   399	        attempted here.
   400	        """
   401	        if self._path_id is None:
   402	            has_link = self.cable_id is not None or getattr(self, 'wireless_link_id', None) is not None
   403	            if self.pk and has_link:
   404	                self.refresh_from_db(fields=['_path'])
   405	
   406	            if self._path_id is None:
   407	                return None
   408	
   409	        try:
   410	            return self._path
   411	        except ObjectDoesNotExist:
   412	            # Refresh only the denormalized FK instead of the whole model.
   413	            # The expected problem here is in-memory staleness during path
   414	            # rebuilds, not persistent database corruption.
   415	            self.refresh_from_db(fields=['_path'])
   416	            return self._path if self._path_id else None
   417	
   418	    @cached_property
   419	    def connected_endpoints(self):
   420	        """
   421	        Caching accessor for the attached CablePath's destinations (if any).
   422	
   423	        Always route through `path` so stale in-memory `_path` references are
   424	        repaired before we cache the result for the lifetime of this instance.
   425	        """
   426	        if cable_path := self.path:
   427	            return cable_path.destinations
   428	        return []
   429

   526	    def clean(self):
   527	        super().clean()

   530	            if self.allocated_draw > self.maximum_draw:
   531	                raise ValidationError({

   537	    def get_downstream_powerports(self, leg=None):
   538	        """
   539	        Return a queryset of all PowerPorts connected via cable to a child PowerOutlet. For example, in the topology
   540	        below, PP1.get_downstream_powerports() would return PP2-4.
   541	
   542	               ---- PO1 <---> PP2
   543	             /
   544	        PP1 ------- PO2 <---> PP3
   545	             \
   546	               ---- PO3 <---> PP4
   547	
   548	        """
   549	        poweroutlets = self.poweroutlets.filter(cable__isnull=False)
   550	        if leg:
   551	            poweroutlets = poweroutlets.filter(feed_leg=leg)
   552	        if not poweroutlets:
   553	            return PowerPort.objects.none()
   554	
   555	        q = Q()
   556	        for poweroutlet in poweroutlets:
   557	            q |= Q(
   558	                cable=poweroutlet.cable,
   559	                cable_end=poweroutlet.opposite_cable_end
   560	            )
   561	
   562	        return PowerPort.objects.filter(q)
   563	
   564	    def get_power_draw(self, _seen=None):
   565	        """
   566	        Return the allocated and maximum power draw (in VA) and child PowerOutlet count for this PowerPort.
   567	        """
   568	        from dcim.models import PowerFeed
   569	
   570	        # Calculate aggregate draw of all child power outlets if no numbers have been defined manually
   571	        if self.allocated_draw is None and self.maximum_draw is None:
   572	
   573	            def _aggregate(powerports, seen):
   574	                # Recursively resolve the draw for each downstream PowerPort. Using the per-port value
   575	                # (rather than a SQL aggregate over allocated_draw/maximum_draw) allows the draw to
   576	                # propagate through intermediate auto-mode PowerPorts, e.g. PDU-internal fuse chains.
   577	                # `seen` tracks visited PowerPorts to prevent infinite recursion if the topology
   578	                # happens to form a cycle.
   579	                allocated_total = 0
   580	                maximum_total = 0
   581	                for powerport in powerports:
   582	                    if powerport.pk in seen:
   583	                        continue
   584	                    seen.add(powerport.pk)
   585	                    draw = powerport.get_power_draw(_seen=seen)
   586	                    allocated_total += draw['allocated']
   587	                    maximum_total += draw['maximum']
   588	                return allocated_total, maximum_total
   589	
   590	            # Seed each _aggregate() call with a fresh copy of the inherited visited set so the full
   591	            # and per-leg aggregations are independent. Otherwise, ports visited during the full
   592	            # aggregation would be skipped during the per-leg passes.
   593	            base_seen = set(_seen) if _seen else set()
   594	            base_seen.add(self.pk)
   595	
   596	            allocated, maximum = _aggregate(self.get_downstream_powerports(), set(base_seen))
   597	            ret = {
   598	                'allocated': allocated,
   599	                'maximum': maximum,
   600	                'outlet_count': self.poweroutlets.count(),
   601	                'legs': [],
   602	            }
   603	
   604	            # Calculate per-leg aggregates for three-phase power feeds
   605	            if len(self.link_peers) == 1 and isinstance(self.link_peers[0], PowerFeed) and \
   606	                    self.link_peers[0].phase == PowerFeedPhaseChoices.PHASE_3PHASE:
   607	                for leg, leg_name in PowerOutletFeedLegChoices:
   608	                    leg_allocated, leg_maximum = _aggregate(
   609	                        self.get_downstream_powerports(leg=leg), set(base_seen)
   610	                    )
   611	                    ret['legs'].append({
   612	                        'name': leg_name,
   613	                        'allocated': leg_allocated,
   614	                        'maximum': leg_maximum,
   615	                        'outlet_count': self.poweroutlets.filter(feed_leg=leg).count(),
   616	                    })
   617	
   618	            return ret
   619	
   620	        # Default to administratively defined values
   621	        return {
   622	            'allocated': self.allocated_draw or 0,
   623	            'maximum': self.maximum_draw or 0,
   624	            'outlet_count': self.poweroutlets.count(),
   625	            'legs': [],
   626	        }

  1029	        # An interface's parent must belong to the same device or virtual chassis

  1037	            if self.parent.device.virtual_chassis != self.device.virtual_chassis:

  1045	                        virtual_chassis=self.device.virtual_chassis
  1046	                    )
  1047	                })
  1048	
  1049	        # Bridge validation
  1050	
  1051	        # A bridged interface belongs to the same device or virtual chassis
  1052	        if self.bridge and self.bridge.device != self.device:
  1053	            if self.device.virtual_chassis is None:

  1059	            if self.bridge.device.virtual_chassis != self.device.virtual_chassis:

  1064	                    ).format(

  1066	                    )
  1067	                })
  1068	
  1069	        # LAG validation

  1071	        # A virtual interface cannot have a parent LAG

  1074	
  1075	        # A LAG interface cannot be its own parent

  1078	
  1079	        # An interface's LAG must belong to the same device or virtual chassis
  1080	        if self.lag and self.lag.device != self.device:
  1081	            if self.device.virtual_chassis is None:
  1082	                raise ValidationError({

  1085	                    ).format(lag=self.lag, device=self.lag.device)
  1086	                })
  1087	            if self.lag.device.virtual_chassis != self.device.virtual_chassis:
  1088	                raise ValidationError({

  1090	                        "The selected LAG interface ({lag}) belongs to {device}, which is not part of virtual chassis "

  1094	                })

  1171	    @cached_property
  1172	    def link_peers(self):
  1173	        if self.cable:

  1177	            if self.wireless_link.interface_a == self:

  1179	            return [self.wireless_link.interface_a]
  1180	        return []

  1331	class ModuleBayManager(TreeManager):
  1332	    """
  1333	    Order ModuleBays by the natural-sort name of each tree's root, then by MPTT
  1334	    left value within the tree. Combined with the root-insert bypass in
  1335	    ModuleBay.save(), this lets us keep MPTTMeta.order_insertion_by for cheap
  1336	    intra-tree sibling placement while skipping the global tree_id renumbering
  1337	    it would otherwise perform on every root insert.
  1338	    """
  1339	    def get_queryset(self, *args, **kwargs):
  1340	        # Use the raw manager to avoid recursing through this get_queryset() when
  1341	        # building the annotation subquery.
  1342	        root_name = self.model._objects_raw.filter(
  1343	            tree_id=models.OuterRef('tree_id'),
  1344	            level=0,
  1345	        ).values('name')[:1]
  1346	        return super().get_queryset(*args, **kwargs).annotate(
  1347	            _root_name=models.Subquery(
  1348	                root_name,
  1349	                output_field=models.CharField(db_collation='natural_sort'),
  1350	            )
  1351	        ).order_by('_root_name', 'lft')

  1398	    class MPTTMeta:
  1399	        # Used for placing siblings within a single tree at insert time. Costs
  1400	        # are bounded to that tree's rows. Cross-tree (root) insertion is
  1401	        # handled in save() to avoid the O(N) tree_id shift this would trigger.

   413	class DeviceViewSet(
   414	    SequentialBulkCreatesMixin,
   415	    ConfigContextQuerySetMixin,
   416	    RenderConfigMixin,
   417	    NetBoxModelViewSet
   418	):
   419	    queryset = Device.objects.prefetch_related(
   420	        'device_type__manufacturer',  # Referenced by Device.__str__() for unnamed devices

   426	    def get_serializer_class(self):
   427	        """
   428	        Select the specific serializer based on the request context.
   429	
   430	        If the `brief` query param equates to True, return the NestedDeviceSerializer
   431	
   432	        If the `exclude` query param includes `config_context` as a value, return the DeviceSerializer
   433	
   434	        Else, return the DeviceWithConfigContextSerializer
   435	        """
   436	        request = self.get_serializer_context()['request']
   437	        if self.brief or 'config_context' in request.query_params.get('exclude', []):
   438	            return serializers.DeviceSerializer
   439	
   440	        return serializers.DeviceWithConfigContextSerializer

   635	
   636	class ConnectedDeviceViewSet(ViewSet):
   637	    """
   638	    This endpoint allows a user to determine what device (if any) is connected to a given peer device and peer
   639	    interface. This is useful in a situation where a device boots with no configuration, but can detect its neighbors
   640	    via a protocol such as LLDP. Two query parameters must be included in the request:
   641	
   642	    * `peer_device`: The name of the peer device
   643	    * `peer_interface`: The name of the peer interface
   644	    """
   645	    permission_classes = [IsAuthenticatedOrLoginNotRequired]
   646	    _device_param = OpenApiParameter(
   647	        name='peer_device',
   648	        location='query',
   649	        description='The name of the peer device',
   650	        required=True,
   651	        type=OpenApiTypes.STR
   652	    )
   653	    _interface_param = OpenApiParameter(
   654	        name='peer_interface',
   655	        location='query',
   656	        description='The name of the peer interface',
   657	        required=True,
   658	        type=OpenApiTypes.STR
   659	    )
   660	    serializer_class = serializers.DeviceSerializer
   661	
   662	    def get_view_name(self):
   663	        return "Connected Device Locator"
   664	
   665	    @extend_schema(
   666	        parameters=[_device_param, _interface_param],
   667	        responses={200: serializers.DeviceSerializer}
   668	    )
   669	    def list(self, request):
   670	
   671	        peer_device_name = request.query_params.get(self._device_param.name)
   672	        peer_interface_name = request.query_params.get(self._interface_param.name)
   673	
   674	        if not peer_device_name or not peer_interface_name:
   675	            raise MissingFilterException(detail='Request must include "peer_device" and "peer_interface" filters.')
   676	
   677	        # Determine local endpoint from peer interface's connection
   678	        peer_device = get_object_or_404(
   679	            Device.objects.restrict(request.user, 'view'),
   680	            name=peer_device_name
   681	        )
   682	        peer_interface = get_object_or_404(
   683	            Interface.objects.restrict(request.user, 'view'),
   684	            device=peer_device,
   685	            name=peer_interface_name
   686	        )
   687	        endpoints = peer_interface.connected_endpoints
   688	
   689	        # If an Interface, return the parent device
   690	        if endpoints and type(endpoints[0]) is Interface:
   691	            device = get_object_or_404(
   692	                Device.objects.restrict(request.user, 'view'),
   693	                pk=endpoints[0].device_id
   694	            )
   695	            return Response(serializers.DeviceSerializer(device, context={'request': request}).data)
   696	
   697	        # Connected endpoint is none or not an Interface
   698	        raise Http404

   116	    @extend_schema_field(NestedDeviceSerializer(allow_null=True))
   117	    def get_parent_device(self, obj):
   118	        try:

   120	        except DeviceBay.DoesNotExist:
   121	            return None
   122	        context = {'request': self.context['request']}
   123	        data = NestedDeviceSerializer(instance=device_bay.device, context=context).data
   124	        data['device_bay'] = NestedDeviceBaySerializer(instance=device_bay, context=context).data

   184	class ModuleSerializer(PrimaryModelSerializer):
   185	    device = DeviceSerializer(nested=True)
   186	    module_bay = NestedModuleBaySerializer()
   187	    module_type = ModuleTypeSerializer(nested=True)
   188	    status = ChoiceField(choices=ModuleStatusChoices, required=False)
   189	    replicate_components = serializers.BooleanField(
   190	        required=False,
   191	        default=True,
   192	        write_only=True,
   193	        label=_('Replicate components'),
   194	        help_text=_('Automatically populate components associated with this module type (default: true)')
   195	    )
   196	    adopt_components = serializers.BooleanField(
   197	        required=False,
   198	        default=False,
   199	        write_only=True,
   200	        label=_('Adopt components'),
   201	        help_text=_('Adopt already existing components')
   202	    )
   203	
   204	    class Meta:
   205	        model = Module
   206	        fields = [
   207	            'id', 'url', 'display_url', 'display', 'device', 'module_bay', 'module_type', 'status', 'serial',
   208	            'asset_tag', 'description', 'owner', 'comments', 'tags', 'custom_fields', 'created', 'last_updated',
   209	            'replicate_components', 'adopt_components',
   210	        ]
   211	        brief_fields = ('id', 'url', 'display', 'device', 'module_bay', 'module_type', 'description')
   212	
   213	    def validate(self, data):
   214	        # When used as a nested serializer (e.g. as the `module` field on device component
   215	        # serializers), `data` is already a resolved Module instance — skip our custom logic.
   216	        if self.nested:
   217	            return super().validate(data)
   218	
   219	        # Pop write-only transient fields before ValidatedModelSerializer tries to
   220	        # construct a Module instance for full_clean(); restore them afterwards.
   221	        replicate_components = data.pop('replicate_components', True)
   222	        adopt_components = data.pop('adopt_components', False)
   223	        data = super().validate(data)
   224	
   225	        # For updates these fields are not meaningful; omit them from validated_data so that
   226	        # ModelSerializer.update() does not set unexpected attributes on the instance.
   227	        if self.instance:
   228	            return data
   229	
   230	        # Always pass the flags to create() so it can set the correct private attributes.
   231	        data['replicate_components'] = replicate_components
   232	        data['adopt_components'] = adopt_components
   233	
   234	        # Skip conflict checks when no component operations are requested.
   235	        if not replicate_components and not adopt_components:
   236	            return data
   237	
   238	        device = data.get('device')
   239	        module_type = data.get('module_type')
   240	        module_bay = data.get('module_bay')
   241	
   242	        # Required-field validation fires separately; skip here if any are missing.
   243	        if not all([device, module_type, module_bay]):
   244	            return data
   245	
   246	        positions = get_module_bay_positions(module_bay)
   247	
   248	        for templates_attr, component_attr in [
   249	            ('consoleporttemplates', 'consoleports'),
   250	            ('consoleserverporttemplates', 'consoleserverports'),
   251	            ('interfacetemplates', 'interfaces'),
   252	            ('powerporttemplates', 'powerports'),
   253	            ('poweroutlettemplates', 'poweroutlets'),
   254	            ('rearporttemplates', 'rearports'),
   255	            ('frontporttemplates', 'frontports'),
   256	        ]:
   257	            installed_components = {
   258	                component.name: component
   259	                for component in getattr(device, component_attr).all()
   260	            }
   261	
   262	            for template in getattr(module_type, templates_attr).all():
   263	                resolved_name = template.name
   264	                if MODULE_TOKEN in template.name:
   265	                    if not module_bay.position:
   266	                        raise serializers.ValidationError(
   267	                            _("Cannot install module with placeholder values in a module bay with no position defined.")
   268	                        )
   269	                    try:
   270	                        resolved_name = resolve_module_placeholder(template.name, positions)
   271	                    except ValueError as e:
   272	                        raise serializers.ValidationError(str(e))
   273	
   274	                existing_item = installed_components.get(resolved_name)
   275	
   276	                if adopt_components and existing_item and existing_item.module:
   277	                    raise serializers.ValidationError(
   278	                        _("Cannot adopt {model} {name} as it already belongs to a module").format(
   279	                            model=template.component_model.__name__,
   280	                            name=resolved_name
   281	                        )
   282	                    )
   283	
   284	                if not adopt_components and resolved_name in installed_components:
   285	                    raise serializers.ValidationError(
   286	                        _("A {model} named {name} already exists").format(
   287	                            model=template.component_model.__name__,
   288	                            name=resolved_name
   289	                        )
   290	                    )
   291	
   292	        return data
   293	
   294	    def create(self, validated_data):
   295	        replicate_components = validated_data.pop('replicate_components', True)
   296	        adopt_components = validated_data.pop('adopt_components', False)
   297	
   298	        # Tags are handled after save; pop them here to pass to _save_tags()
   299	        tags = validated_data.pop('tags', None)
   300	
   301	        # _adopt_components and _disable_replication must be set on the instance before
   302	        # save() is called, so we cannot delegate to super().create() here.
   303	        instance = self.Meta.model(**validated_data)
   304	        if adopt_components:
   305	            instance._adopt_components = True
   306	        if not replicate_components:
   307	            instance._disable_replication = True
   308	        instance.save()
   309	
   310	        if tags is not None:
   311	            self._save_tags(instance, tags)
   312	
   313	        return instance

  1484	    def search(self, queryset, name, value):
  1485	        if not value.strip():
  1486	            return queryset
  1487	        qs_filter = (
  1488	            Q(name__icontains=value) |
  1489	            Q(virtual_chassis__name__icontains=value) |
  1490	            Q(serial__icontains=value.strip()) |
  1491	            Q(asset_tag__icontains=value.strip()) |
  1492	            Q(description__icontains=value.strip()) |
  1493	            Q(comments__icontains=value)
  1494	        )
  1495	        # If the given value looks like an IP address, look for primary IPv4/IPv6 assignments
  1496	        try:
  1497	            ipaddress = netaddr.IPNetwork(value)
  1498	            if ipaddress.version == 4:
  1499	                qs_filter |= Q(primary_ip4__address__host__inet=ipaddress.ip)
  1500	            elif ipaddress.version == 6:
  1501	                qs_filter |= Q(primary_ip6__address__host__inet=ipaddress.ip)
  1502	        except (AddrFormatError, ValueError):
  1503	            pass
  1504	        return queryset.filter(qs_filter)
  1505

  1507	        params = Q(primary_ip4__isnull=False) | Q(primary_ip6__isnull=False)
  1508	        if value:
  1509	            return queryset.filter(params)
  1510	        return queryset.exclude(params)

  1513	        params = Q(oob_ip__isnull=False)
  1514	        if value:

  2087	    def search(self, queryset, name, value):
  2088	        if not value.strip():
  2089	            return queryset
  2090	        qs_filter = (
  2091	            Q(mac_address__icontains=value) |
  2092	            Q(description__icontains=value)
  2093	        )
  2094	        return queryset.filter(qs_filter)
  2095	
  2096	    def filter_device(self, queryset, name, value):
  2097	        devices = Device.objects.filter(**{f'{name}__in': value})
  2098	        if not devices.exists():
  2099	            return queryset.none()
  2100	        interface_ids = []
  2101	        for device in devices:
  2102	            interface_ids.extend(device.vc_interfaces().values_list('id', flat=True))
  2103	        return queryset.filter(
  2104	            interface__in=interface_ids
  2105	        )
  2106	
  2107	    def filter_virtual_machine(self, queryset, name, value):
  2108	        virtual_machines = VirtualMachine.objects.filter(**{f'{name}__in': value})
  2109	        if not virtual_machines.exists():
  2110	            return queryset.none()
  2111	        interface_ids = []
  2112	        for vm in virtual_machines:
  2113	            interface_ids.extend(vm.interfaces.values_list('id', flat=True))
  2114	        return queryset.filter(
  2115	            vminterface__in=interface_ids
  2116	        )
  2117	
  2118	    def filter_assigned(self, queryset, name, value):
  2119	        params = {
  2120	            'assigned_object_type__isnull': True,
  2121	            'assigned_object_id__isnull': True,
  2122	        }
  2123	        if value:
  2124	            return queryset.exclude(**params)
  2125	        return queryset.filter(**params)
  2126	
  2127	    def filter_primary(self, queryset, name, value):
  2128	        interface_mac_ids = Interface.objects.filter(primary_mac_address_id__isnull=False).values_list(
  2129	            'primary_mac_address_id', flat=True
  2130	        )
  2131	        vminterface_mac_ids = VMInterface.objects.filter(primary_mac_address_id__isnull=False).values_list(
  2132	            'primary_mac_address_id', flat=True
  2133	        )
  2134	        query = Q(pk__in=interface_mac_ids) | Q(pk__in=vminterface_mac_ids)
  2135	        if value:
  2136	            return queryset.filter(query)
  2137	        return queryset.exclude(query)

  1940	class PathEndpointFilterSet(django_filters.FilterSet):
  1941	    connected = django_filters.BooleanFilter(

  1946	        if value:
  1947	            return queryset.filter(_path__is_active=True, _path__is_complete=True)
  1948	        return queryset.filter(Q(_path__isnull=True) | Q(_path__is_active=False) | Q(_path__is_complete=False))

The DCIM core is the largest blast radius: device creation instantiates components, component forms/API assume immutable `device`, VDCs and virtual chassis have their own device relations, and rack/cable views infer device placement from component parents. I’m checking the non-DCIM production dependents now so the final map doesn’t collapse to “DCIM only.”

   104	    """
   105	    A virtual machine which runs on a Cluster or a standalone Device.
   106	
   107	    Each VM must be placed in at least one of three ways:
   108	
   109	    1. Assigned to a Site alone (e.g. for logical grouping without a specific host).
   110	    2. Assigned to a Cluster and optionally pinned to a host Device within that cluster.
   111	    3. Assigned directly to a standalone Device (one that does not belong to any cluster).
   112	
   113	    When a Cluster or Device is set, the Site is automatically inherited if not explicitly provided.
   114	    If a Device belongs to a Cluster, the Cluster must also be specified on the VM.
   115	    """

   289	        super().clean()

   291	        # Must be assigned to a site, cluster, and/or device
   292	        if not self.site and not self.cluster and not self.device:
   293	            raise ValidationError(
   294	                _('A virtual machine must be assigned to a site, cluster, or device.')
   295	            )
   296	
   297	        # Validate site for cluster & VM
   298	        if self.cluster and self.site and self.cluster._site and self.cluster._site != self.site:
   299	            raise ValidationError({
   300	                'cluster': _(
   301	                    'The selected cluster ({cluster}) is not assigned to this site ({site}).'
   302	                ).format(cluster=self.cluster, site=self.site)
   303	            })
   304	
   305	        # Validate site for the device & VM (when the device is standalone)
   306	        if self.device and self.site and self.device.site and self.device.site != self.site:
   307	            raise ValidationError({
   308	                'site': _(
   309	                    'The selected device ({device}) is not assigned to this site ({site}).'
   310	                ).format(device=self.device, site=self.site)
   311	            })
   312	
   313	        # Direct device assignment is only for standalone hosts. If the selected
   314	        # device already belongs to a cluster, require that cluster explicitly.
   315	        if self.device and not self.cluster and self.device.cluster:
   316	            raise ValidationError({
   317	                'cluster': _(
   318	                    "Must specify the assigned device's cluster ({cluster}) when assigning host device {device}."
   319	                ).format(cluster=self.device.cluster, device=self.device)
   320	            })
   321	
   322	        # Validate assigned cluster device
   323	        if self.device and self.cluster and self.device.cluster_id != self.cluster_id:
   324	            raise ValidationError({
   325	                'device': _(
   326	                    "The selected device ({device}) is not assigned to this cluster ({cluster})."
   327	                ).format(device=self.device, cluster=self.cluster)
   328	            })
   329	
   330	        # Validate aggregate disk size
   331	        if not self._state.adding:
   332	            total_disk = self.virtualdisks.aggregate(Sum('size', default=0))['size__sum']
   333	            if total_disk and self.disk is None:
   334	                self.disk = total_disk
   335	            elif total_disk and self.disk != total_disk:
   336	                raise ValidationError({
   337	                    'disk': _(
   338	                        "The specified disk size ({size}) must match the aggregate size of assigned virtual disks "
   339	                        "({total_size})."
   340	                    ).format(size=self.disk, total_size=total_disk)
   341	                })
   342	
   343	        # Validate primary IP addresses
   344	        interfaces = self.interfaces.all() if self.pk else None
   345	        for family in (4, 6):

   355	                if ip.assigned_object in interfaces:

   377	    def apply_type_defaults(self):

   945	    Interfaces can have zero or more IPAddresses assigned to them.

  1062	            if start_ip <= end_ip:

  1064	                available_ips -= netaddr.IPSet([
  1065	                    address.ip for address in IPAddress.objects.filter(

   453	        ipaddress = super().save(*args, **kwargs)

   455	        # Assign/clear this IPAddress as the primary for the associated Device/VirtualMachine.
   456	        interface = self.instance.assigned_object

   458	            parent = interface.parent_object
   459	            parent.snapshot()
   460	            if self.cleaned_data['primary_for_parent']:
   461	                if ipaddress.address.version == 4:

   463	                else:

   465	                parent.save()
   466	            elif ipaddress.address.version == 4 and parent.primary_ip4 == ipaddress:
   467	                parent.primary_ip4 = None
   468	                parent.save()
   469	            elif ipaddress.address.version == 6 and parent.primary_ip6 == ipaddress:
   470	                parent.primary_ip6 = None
   471	                parent.save()
   472	
   473	        # Assign/clear this IPAddress as the OOB for the associated Device
   474	        if type(interface) is Interface:

   479	                parent.save()
   480	            elif parent.oob_ip == ipaddress:
   481	                parent.oob_ip = None
   482	                parent.save()
   483	
   484	        return ipaddress

    10	def update_parents_children(prefix):
    11	    """
    12	    Update depth on prefix & containing prefixes
    13	    """
    14	    parents = prefix.get_parents(include_self=True).annotate_hierarchy()
    15	    for parent in parents:
    16	        parent._children = parent.hierarchy_children
    17	    Prefix.objects.bulk_update(parents, ['_children'], batch_size=100)

    20	def update_children_depth(prefix):
    21	    """
    22	    Update children count on prefix & contained prefixes
    23	    """
    24	    children = prefix.get_children(include_self=True).annotate_hierarchy()
    25	    for child in children:
    26	        child._depth = child.hierarchy_depth
    27	    Prefix.objects.bulk_update(children, ['_depth'], batch_size=100)

    30	@receiver(post_save, sender=Prefix)
    31	def handle_prefix_saved(instance, created, **kwargs):
    32	
    33	    # Prefix has changed (or new instance has been created)
    34	    if created or instance.vrf_id != instance._vrf_id or instance.prefix != instance._prefix:
    35	
    36	        update_parents_children(instance)
    37	        update_children_depth(instance)
    38	
    39	        # If this is not a new prefix, clean up parent/children of previous prefix
    40	        if not created:
    41	            old_prefix = Prefix(vrf_id=instance._vrf_id, prefix=instance._prefix)
    42	            update_parents_children(old_prefix)
    43	            update_children_depth(old_prefix)
    44

    47	def handle_prefix_deleted(instance, **kwargs):
    48	
    49	    update_parents_children(instance)

    53	@receiver(pre_delete, sender=IPAddress)
    54	def clear_primary_ip(instance, **kwargs):
    55	    """
    56	    When an IPAddress is deleted, trigger save() on any Devices/VirtualMachines for which it was a primary IP.
    57	    """
    58	    field_name = f'primary_ip{instance.family}'
    59	    if device := Device.objects.filter(**{field_name: instance}).first():
    60	        device.snapshot()
    61	        setattr(device, field_name, None)
    62	        device.save()
    63	    if virtualmachine := VirtualMachine.objects.filter(**{field_name: instance}).first():
    64	        virtualmachine.snapshot()
    65	        setattr(virtualmachine, field_name, None)
    66	        virtualmachine.save()
    67	
    68	
    69	@receiver(pre_delete, sender=IPAddress)
    70	def clear_oob_ip(instance, **kwargs):
    71	    """
    72	    When an IPAddress is deleted, trigger save() on any Devices for which it was a OOB IP.
    73	    """
    74	    if device := Device.objects.filter(oob_ip=instance).first():
    75	        device.snapshot()
    76	        device.oob_ip = None
    77	        device.save()

   167	    def filter_device(self, field, value, prefix) -> Q:
   168	        """Helper to standardize logic for device and device_id filters"""
   169	        devices = Device.objects.filter(**{f'{field}__in': value})
   170	        interface_ids = []
   171	        for device in devices:
   172	            interface_ids.extend(device.vc_interfaces().values_list('id', flat=True))
   173	        return Q(**{f"{prefix}interface_id__in": interface_ids})

    17	
    18	    def get_for_object(self, obj, aggregate_data=False):
    19	        """
    20	        Return all applicable ConfigContexts for a given object. Only active ConfigContexts will be included.
    21	
    22	        Args:
    23	          aggregate_data: If True, use the JSONBAgg aggregate function to return only the list of JSON data objects
    24	        """

    27	        device_type = getattr(obj, 'device_type', None)
    28	        location = getattr(obj, 'location', None)
    29	        locations = location.get_ancestors(include_self=True) if location else []
    30	
    31	        # Get assigned cluster, group, and type (if any)
    32	        cluster = getattr(obj, 'cluster', None)
    33	        cluster_type = getattr(cluster, 'type', None)
    34	        cluster_group = getattr(cluster, 'group', None)
    35	
    36	        # Get the group of the assigned tenant, if any
    37	        tenant_group = obj.tenant.group if obj.tenant else None
    38	
    39	        # Match against the directly assigned region as well as any parent regions.
    40	        region = getattr(obj.site, 'region', None)
    41	        regions = region.get_ancestors(include_self=True) if region else []
    42	
    43	        # Match against the directly assigned site group as well as any parent site groups.
    44	        sitegroup = getattr(obj.site, 'group', None)
    45	        sitegroups = sitegroup.get_ancestors(include_self=True) if sitegroup else []
    46	
    47	        # Match against the directly assigned role as well as any parent roles.
    48	        device_roles = obj.role.get_ancestors(include_self=True) if obj.role else []
    49	
    50	        # Match against the directly assigned platform as well as any parent platforms.
    51	        platform = getattr(obj, 'platform', None)
    52	        platforms = platform.get_ancestors(include_self=True) if platform else []
    53	
    54	        queryset = self.filter(
    55	            Q(regions__in=regions) | Q(regions=None),
    56	            Q(site_groups__in=sitegroups) | Q(site_groups=None),
    57	            Q(sites=obj.site) | Q(sites=None),
    58	            Q(locations__in=locations) | Q(locations=None),
    59	            Q(device_types=device_type) | Q(device_types=None),
    60	            Q(roles__in=device_roles) | Q(roles=None),

    64	            Q(clusters=cluster) | Q(clusters=None),
    65	            Q(tenant_groups=tenant_group) | Q(tenant_groups=None),

    67	            Q(tags__slug__in=obj.tags.slugs()) | Q(tags=None),

    79	class ConfigContextModelQuerySet(RestrictedQuerySet):
    80	    """
    81	    QuerySet manager used by models which support ConfigContext (device and virtual machine).
    82	
    83	    Includes a method which appends an annotation of aggregated config context JSON data objects. This is
    84	    implemented as a subquery which performs all the joins necessary to filter relevant config context objects.
    85	    This offers a substantial performance gain over ConfigContextQuerySet.get_for_object() when dealing with
    86	    multiple objects. This allows the annotation to be entirely optional.
    87	    """
    88	    def annotate_config_context_data(self):
    89	        """
    90	        Attach the subquery annotation to the base queryset
    91	        """
    92	        from extras.models import ConfigContext
    93	        return self.annotate(
    94	            config_context_data=Subquery(
    95	                ConfigContext.objects.filter(
    96	                    self._get_config_context_filters()
    97	                ).annotate(
    98	                    _data=EmptyGroupByJSONBAgg('data', order_by=['weight', 'name'])
    99	                ).values("_data").order_by()
   100	            )
   101	        )
   102	
   103	    def _get_config_context_filters(self):
   104	        # Construct the set of Q objects for the specific object types
   105	        tag_query_filters = {
   106	            "object_id": OuterRef(OuterRef('pk')),
   107	            "content_type__app_label": self.model._meta.app_label,
   108	            "content_type__model": self.model._meta.model_name
   109	        }
   110	        base_query = Q(
   111	            Q(cluster_types=OuterRef('cluster__type')) | Q(cluster_types=None),
   112	            Q(cluster_groups=OuterRef('cluster__group')) | Q(cluster_groups=None),
   113	            Q(clusters=OuterRef('cluster')) | Q(clusters=None),
   114	            Q(tenant_groups=OuterRef('tenant__group')) | Q(tenant_groups=None),
   115	            Q(tenants=OuterRef('tenant')) | Q(tenants=None),
   116	            Q(sites=OuterRef('site')) | Q(sites=None),
   117	            Q(
   118	                tags__pk__in=Subquery(
   119	                    TaggedItem.objects.filter(
   120	                        **tag_query_filters
   121	                    ).values_list(
   122	                        'tag_id',
   123	                        flat=True
   124	                    ).distinct()
   125	                )
   126	            ) | Q(tags=None),
   127	            is_active=True,
   128	        )
   129	
   130	        # Apply Location & DeviceType filters only for VirtualMachines
   131	        if self.model._meta.model_name == 'device':
   132	            base_query.add(
   133	                (Q(
   134	                    locations__tree_id=OuterRef('location__tree_id'),
   135	                    locations__level__lte=OuterRef('location__level'),
   136	                    locations__lft__lte=OuterRef('location__lft'),
   137	                    locations__rght__gte=OuterRef('location__rght'),
   138	                ) | Q(locations=None)),
   139	                Q.AND
   140	            )
   141	            base_query.add((Q(device_types=OuterRef('device_type')) | Q(device_types=None)), Q.AND)
   142	        elif self.model._meta.model_name == 'virtualmachine':
   143	            base_query.add(Q(locations=None), Q.AND)
   144	            base_query.add(Q(device_types=None), Q.AND)

    58	@receiver(post_save, sender=Location)
    59	def handle_location_site_change(instance, created, **kwargs):
    60	    """
    61	    Update child objects if Site assignment has changed. We intentionally recurse through each child
    62	    object instead of calling update() on the QuerySet to ensure the proper change records get created for each.
    63	    """
    64	    if not created:
    65	        instance.get_descendants().update(site=instance.site)
    66	        locations = instance.get_descendants(include_self=True).values_list('pk', flat=True)
    67	        Rack.objects.filter(location__in=locations).update(site=instance.site)
    68	        Device.objects.filter(location__in=locations).update(site=instance.site)
    69	        PowerPanel.objects.filter(location__in=locations).update(site=instance.site)
    70	        CableTermination.objects.filter(_location__in=locations).update(_site=instance.site)
    71	        # Update component models for devices in these locations
    72	        for model in COMPONENT_MODELS:
    73	            model.objects.filter(device__location__in=locations).update(_site=instance.site)

   122	def update_virtualchassis_member_search_cache(instance, created, raw=False, update_fields=None, **kwargs):
   123	    """
   124	    Refresh the search cache for member Devices when a VirtualChassis is renamed. DeviceIndex caches
   125	    virtual_chassis as its string value, so a rename would otherwise leave stale CachedValue entries.
   126	    """
   127	    if raw or created:

   129	    # The VC name is the only VC attribute cached on member Devices; skip saves that can't change it.
   130	    if update_fields is not None and 'name' not in update_fields:

   132	    search_backend.cache(
